summary: This is an advice column installment where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from racial profiling and marijuana arrests to teaching calculus with limited resources to academic job negotiations. The column opens with a plea for more reader questions. The most substantive exchange addresses a reader who questioned O'Neil's claim about racial bias in marijuana arrests, to which she responds by pointing out the systemic inequalities in policing and privacy that lead to disparate arrest rates. She also discusses teaching strategies for underprepared students and advises an academic on when to mention competing job offers.
key_points: Racial disparities in marijuana arrests reflect systemic racism and unequal policing, not differences in drug use or 'education about hiding drugs'
White suburban kids have privacy to smoke pot at home while Black kids often must use public spaces, leading to higher arrest exposure
The anecdote about Stuyvesant parents negotiating with cops to avoid arrests for their kids illustrates how privilege operates in the justice system
For teaching underprepared students, interactive problem-solving during class time is more valuable than pure lecturing
In academic job negotiations, don't mention competing offers until they're firm
money_quotes: Clearly black kids are much more educated about cops than the average white kids. Are you kidding?
White kids smoke pot in their own rooms in suburbia, and it's harder for them to get caught.
Imagine that deal being made in Harlem.
I wish more things were that informal combined with great.
Keep in mind that the quiet ones will probably be the ones that feel most behind, so make sure you don't just listen to the loud people!
key_insight: Racial disparities in arrest rates are not neutral facts but consequences of systemic inequalities in privacy, policing, and privilege that algorithms would incorrectly treat as natural patterns.
